regions.name: Sakai
regions.summary: Specialized production chains: smiths, sharpeners, brands and retailers often all matter.
notes: Use Sakai as a chain-reading region: who forged it, who sharpened it, which line, which retailer context.
The blacksmith and sharpener relationship is often the point, not a footnote.

nodes.name: Ikeda
nodes.specialty: Sakai carbon forging, traditional single-bevel work and honyaki-adjacent buying context.
nodes.caveat: Exact first name and line attribution should be checked listing by listing.

nodes.name: Nomura
nodes.specialty: Sakai sharpening, bevel geometry and final cutting feel.
nodes.caveat: Added as the Ikeda/Nomura relationship example requested by Adrian; keep exact product claims conservative.

nodes.name: Kenichi Shiraki
nodes.specialty: Sakai carbon forging lineage, honyaki capability and apprenticeship influence.

nodes.name: Satoshi Nakagawa
nodes.specialty: Ginsan, Aogami, Shirogami and honyaki-capable Sakai forging.

nodes.name: Ashi Hamono
nodes.specialty: Thin Sakai laser geometry and clean monosteel performance.

nodes.name: Mitsuaki Takada
nodes.specialty: Thin convex hamaguri-style grinding, Suiboku finish and modern Sakai polish.

nodes.name: Konosuke
nodes.specialty: Sakai curation where smith, sharpener and exact line define the knife.

nodes.name: Morihiro
nodes.specialty: Reference-point Sakai sharpening identity behind original Fujiyama context, with later student-lineage relevance for sharpeners such as Nishida and Tadokoro.

nodes.name: Nishida
nodes.specialty: Sakai sharpener represented here as part of Morihiro student-lineage context and Baba Hamono / Kagekiyo sharpening context.
nodes.caveat: Baba's own site supports in-workshop sharpening for Kagekiyo; Nishida's named role is added from Adrian correction and community trail, so product-specific attribution still needs exact listing checks.

nodes.name: Kazumi Kawakita
nodes.specialty: Sakai sharpening lineage node for the Kawakita to Morihiro relationship.
nodes.caveat: Added from Adrian's requested graph note; attach a public source trail before making stronger product claims.

nodes.name: Yoshikazu Tanaka
nodes.specialty: Sakai City master blacksmith with 50-plus years at the forge; a carbon, Blue #1, White steel, Damascus and honyaki-adjacent reference point.

nodes.name: Kenji Togashi
nodes.specialty: Sakai blacksmith frequently discussed around honyaki, carbon forging and high-end retailer lines.
nodes.caveat: Community-heavy node: check exact retailer and line attribution before treating this as a buying claim.

nodes.name: Tadokoro Hamono
nodes.specialty: Sakai sharpening and finishing house, represented here with Morihiro student-lineage context and collaborations with named smiths.
nodes.caveat: Relationship edges are community/retailer leads until exact product pages are attached.

nodes.name: Hitohira
nodes.specialty: Curated Japanese knife brand where hidden and named smith/sharpener pairings matter.

nodes.name: Sakai Kikumori
nodes.specialty: Established Sakai brand and house line context for named smith/sharpener discussion.

nodes.name: Sakai Takayuki
nodes.specialty: Large Sakai brand context for commercial lines, traditional profiles and export-visible knives.

nodes.name: HADO / Fukui
nodes.specialty: Modern Sakai presentation with named craft, Ginsan lines and clean finishing.

nodes.name: Baba Hamono
nodes.specialty: Sakai maker network, Kagekiyo house identity and in-workshop sharpening context.

nodes.name: Kagekiyo
nodes.specialty: Baba Hamono's high-end house line, built around Sakai forged knife tradition, in-workshop hatsuke and Urushi handle work.

nodes.name: Momose Juntetsu
nodes.specialty: Traditional Urushi lacquerware handle craft credited by Baba Hamono for Kagekiyo handles.

edges.label: forging to sharpening
edges.detail: Ikeda is treated as the blacksmith node and Nomura as the sharpener node in the Sakai chain example.

edges.label: apprenticeship lineage
edges.detail: Nakagawa is commonly described as training under Shiraki before founding his own Sakai workshop.

edges.label: Ashi background
edges.detail: Takada's profile is tied to years at Ashi Hamono before founding Takada no Hamono.

edges.label: Yohei = Takada
edges.detail: Yohei is represented as a buyer-facing name tied to Mitsuaki Takada / Takada no Hamono context.

edges.label: Fujiyama sharpening identity
edges.detail: Konosuke's Fujiyama/FM story makes sharpener attribution part of the buyer conversation.

edges.label: student
edges.detail: Morihiro is represented as a student of Kazumi Kawakita in this map.

edges.label: Tanaka / Tadokoro pairing
edges.detail: Community and retailer discussion often treats Tanaka-forged, Tadokoro-finished knives as a distinct buying path.

edges.label: Hitohira / Tanaka lines
edges.detail: Hitohira-Tanaka lines are included as a map trail for named smith and sharpener pairings.

edges.label: Hitohira / Togashi lines
edges.detail: Hitohira-Togashi lines are shown as a buyer-facing collaboration path requiring exact listing checks.

edges.label: Kikumori / Tanaka context
edges.detail: Sakai Kikumori appears in buyer discussions around Tanaka-forged house and named lines; verify by exact product.

edges.label: Takayuki / Togashi context
edges.detail: Sakai Takayuki provides a broad commercial Sakai context; named Togashi connections should be checked listing by listing.

edges.label: modern Sakai brand context
edges.detail: Both show how modern Sakai brands make specialist craft legible to buyers.

edges.label: house line
edges.detail: Kagekiyo is represented as Baba Hamono's original high-end line rather than a separate maker.

edges.label: works at
edges.detail: Nishida is mapped as working at Baba Hamono in the Kagekiyo sharpening context; exact product attribution should still be checked listing by listing.

edges.label: Kagekiyo hatsuke context
edges.detail: Baba describes Kagekiyo hatsuke as happening in its workshop; Nishida is mapped here as the named sharpener context to investigate on exact listings.

edges.label: Urushi handle work
edges.detail: Baba credits Momose Juntetsu for the Urushi lacquered handles used in Kagekiyo context.

edges.label: Tanaka / Kyuzo (Myojin)
edges.detail: Tanaka x Kyuzo is represented as a buyer-facing trail to Naohito Myojin, not as a separate Kyuzo person.

edges.label: Nakagawa / Kyuzo (Myojin)
edges.detail: Nakagawa x Kyuzo is represented as a buyer-facing trail to Naohito Myojin, useful for comparing Nakagawa x Morihiro and other finishes.

edges.label: Togashi / Kyuzo (Myojin)
edges.detail: Togashi x Kyuzo is represented as a community-visible trail to Naohito Myojin; verify exact listing disclosure.

edges.label: Hitohira / Kyuzo (Myojin)
edges.detail: Hitohira is represented as the buyer-facing brand context where Kyuzo/Myojin pairings are often encountered.

edges.label: Morihiro student
edges.detail: Nishida is represented as part of Morihiro student-lineage context in this graph.

edges.label: Morihiro student-lineage
edges.detail: Tadokoro is represented with Morihiro student-lineage context while retaining its own sharpener/collaboration node.

regions.name: Sanjo
regions.summary: Blacksmith-forward shops, robust convexity and workhorse identities.
notes: Sanjo is useful for understanding taller, more powerful double-bevel knives.
Relationships here often look like workshop training or regional influence rather than a Sakai-style smith/sharpener split.

nodes.name: Yoshikane Hamono
nodes.specialty: Sanjo convex grinds, SKD, White #2 and controlled workhorse geometry.

nodes.name: Toshihiro Wakui
nodes.specialty: Small-shop Sanjo forging with Yoshikane influence; JNS documents White #2, Blue #2, Damascus and natural-stone-finished Kokorozashi/Seiryu contexts.

nodes.name: Syuuji Toyama
nodes.specialty: Handmade Sanjo carbon knives, strong food-moving geometry and JNS-exclusive Noborikoi context across Blue, White and honyaki work.

nodes.name: Shigefusa
nodes.specialty: Tokifusa Iizuka and sons in Sanjo; benchmark hand work with Swedish carbon steel, sen finishing, kasumi and in-house Kitaeji cladding.
nodes.caveat: The source trail supports Sanjo workshop and technique context; scarcity and product-specific attribution still need exact listing checks.

nodes.name: Kiyoshi Kato
nodes.specialty: Carbon forging reference point distinct from Echizen's Yoshimi Kato and Sakai blacksmiths; Yoshiaki Fujiwara is the knife-name trail to reconcile by listing.
nodes.caveat: Region corrected from Sakai to Sanjo from Adrian's correction. Japanese Natural Stones identifies Yoshiaki Fujiwara as Kiyoshi Kato and places the workshop in Hokuto, Yamanashi, so region and pseudonym should be reconciled listing by listing.

nodes.name: Munetoshi / Kouichi Tsurumaki
nodes.specialty: Third-generation Sanjo blacksmith with axe, nata and heavy-blade background; rustic water-quenched, hand-laminated White steel work built for toughness.

nodes.name: Kaeru
nodes.specialty: Japanese Natural Stones house line built for practical professional use, usually framed around SLD high-carbon stainless and easy care.
nodes.caveat: House-line node only; do not infer a named blacksmith or sharpener unless a product page states it.

nodes.name: Masashi Yamamoto
nodes.specialty: Sanjo profile language, distinctive grinds and visually expressive lines.

nodes.name: Tadafusa
nodes.specialty: Sanjo workshop making household and professional kitchen knives with a factory shop in Sanjo.

nodes.name: Shinichi Watanabe
nodes.specialty: Sanjo blacksmith making hand-forged custom kitchen knives and professional blades.

nodes.name: Naoki Mazaki
nodes.specialty: Sanjo blacksmith known for evolving grinds, tall profiles and White #2 workhorse discussions.

nodes.name: Hinoura Hamono
nodes.specialty: Sanjo family workshop lineage around Mutsumi and Tsukasa Hinoura.

nodes.name: Mutsumi Hinoura
nodes.specialty: Senior Sanjo smith and father-lineage node for Tsukasa Hinoura.

nodes.name: Tsukasa Hinoura
nodes.specialty: Sanjo blacksmith known for high-finish carbon work, River Jump and family-workshop lineage.

nodes.name: Isamitsu Hamono
nodes.specialty: Small Sanjo workshop node often discussed alongside modern handmade carbon lines.
nodes.caveat: Community-heavy node pending a stronger public workshop/source link.

edges.label: Yoshikane influence
edges.detail: Wakui profiles are commonly framed through time at Yoshikane and Sanjo tool-making context.

edges.label: Sanjo workhorse comparison
edges.detail: Yoshikane and Toyama are useful comparison points for Sanjo power, convexity and carbon care.

edges.label: Sanjo maker context
edges.detail: Both help show that Sanjo is not one grind; it is a region with different shop identities.

edges.label: Sanjo workshop comparison
edges.detail: Yoshikane and Tadafusa are both useful Sanjo workshop references, but the buying context is different.

edges.label: teacher
edges.detail: Toyama is represented as Watanabe's teacher/mentor in this graph; keep product-specific claims tied to exact listings.

edges.label: Sanjo benchmark context
edges.detail: Shigefusa and Toyama are mapped as distinct high-end Sanjo/Niigata reference points, not as the same shop or grind.

edges.label: Sanjo high-end carbon context
edges.detail: Kiyoshi Kato is mapped in the Sanjo/Niigata context, not Sakai; the aliases capture buyer-facing Kato naming around the workhorse/kasumi trail.

edges.label: Sanjo rustic carbon comparison
edges.detail: Munetoshi and Shigefusa are both Sanjo carbon reference points in the JNS trail, but this is a regional comparison, not a shared workshop claim.

edges.label: Yoshikane background
edges.detail: Masashi is placed under Yoshikane as a community/retailer-trail relationship; verify exact biography wording per source.

edges.label: Yoshikane background
edges.detail: Mazaki is commonly discussed through a Yoshikane/Sanjo training background, but the exact phrasing should remain conservative.

edges.label: Hinoura workshop
edges.detail: Mutsumi Hinoura is represented inside the Hinoura Hamono family workshop context.

edges.label: father to son lineage
edges.detail: The Hinoura family line is represented as a direct genealogy relationship in the Sanjo chart.

edges.label: modern Sanjo small-shop context
edges.detail: Mazaki and Isamitsu are paired as modern Sanjo small-shop context, not as a claimed teacher/student relationship.

regions.name: Echizen
regions.summary: Takefu-style maker networks, laminated steels and visually distinct modern lines.
notes: Echizen/Takefu is useful as a cooperative-region map: several recognizable makers share regional infrastructure and training context.
Use exact maker and line before assuming a single Echizen cutting feel.

nodes.name: Takefu Knife Village
nodes.specialty: Regional maker hub for Echizen forged knives and shared craft visibility.

nodes.name: Yu Kurosaki
nodes.specialty: Highly recognizable hammered and patterned finishes with modern export visibility.

nodes.name: Takeshi Saji
nodes.specialty: Expressive Damascus, western handles, R2/SG2 and outdoor-leaning visual language.

nodes.name: Shiro Kamo
nodes.specialty: Accessible handmade carbon and stainless-clad Echizen knives.

nodes.name: Yoshimi Kato
nodes.specialty: Damascus, SG2/R2 and clean modern Echizen lines.

nodes.name: Katsuyasu Kamo
nodes.specialty: Traditional Echizen forging at Kamo Hamono, including forged knives and agricultural blades.

nodes.name: Anryu Knives
nodes.specialty: Takefu Knife Village member workshop in the Echizen maker network.

nodes.name: Kitaoka Knives
nodes.specialty: Takefu Knife Village member workshop tied to traditional Echizen forged knives.

nodes.name: Yuji Totani
nodes.specialty: Sharpening Four representative, traditional craftsman and specialist in edge setting and repair.

nodes.name: Sharpening Four
nodes.specialty: Takefu Knife Village sharpening workshop context for edge setting, repair and finishing.

nodes.name: Okada Knives
nodes.specialty: Takefu Knife Village member workshop in the Echizen production network.

nodes.name: Kamoto Hamono
nodes.specialty: Takefu Knife Village member workshop in the Echizen production network.

nodes.name: Honda Knives
nodes.specialty: Takefu Knife Village member workshop in the Echizen production network.

nodes.name: Yamamoto Knives
nodes.specialty: Takefu Knife Village member workshop in the Echizen production network.

nodes.name: Masakuni Kurosaki
nodes.specialty: Kurosaki family Echizen craft node linked through Takefu Knife Village's STYLE-K listing.

nodes.name: Hatono Hamono
nodes.specialty: Takefu Knife Village member workshop in the Echizen production network.

nodes.name: Takamura Hamono
nodes.specialty: Thin stainless and powdered-steel laser knives from Fukui/Sabae context, often compared with Tojiro as the next performance step.
nodes.caveat: Official source URL still pending; relationship is included as buyer comparison context.

nodes.name: Ryusen Hamono
nodes.specialty: High-finish Fukui stainless and Damascus production with polished fit-and-finish context.

edges.label: Takefu network
edges.detail: Kurosaki is best read inside the broader Takefu/Echizen maker network.

edges.label: Takefu network
edges.detail: Saji is a senior Echizen reference point for expressive Damascus and modern lines.

edges.label: Takefu network
edges.detail: Shiro Kamo helps show the accessible handmade carbon side of the region.

edges.label: Takefu network
edges.detail: Kato belongs in the same regional network but should still be read by exact line and steel.

edges.detail: Kamo Hamono is listed by Takefu Knife Village as a member company.

edges.label: Takefu member
edges.detail: Anryu Knives is listed by Takefu Knife Village as a member company.

edges.label: Takefu member
edges.detail: Kitaoka Knives is listed by Takefu Knife Village as a member company.

edges.detail: Sharpening Four is listed by Takefu Knife Village and gives the graph a confirmed sharpening specialist.

edges.label: Takefu sharpening
edges.detail: Sharpening Four is listed by Takefu Knife Village as a sharpening workshop context.

edges.label: Sharpening Four
edges.detail: Yuji Totani is represented through the Sharpening Four workshop context.

edges.label: Takefu member
edges.detail: Okada Knives is listed by Takefu Knife Village as a member company.

edges.label: Takefu member

edges.label: Fukui stainless context
edges.detail: Takamura is included in broader Fukui/Echizen stainless context, not as a Takefu Knife Village member claim.

edges.label: high-finish stainless context
edges.detail: Takamura and Ryusen help frame thin stainless performance versus polished production fit and finish.

regions.name: Tosa / Kochi
regions.summary: Small-shop forging and polishing context around Myojin, Tamura and Tetsujin-style collaborations.
notes: Use this branch for smith/polisher pairings rather than treating every Tosa knife as the same rustic category.
The Myojin/Tamura/Tetsujin trail is especially useful because polishing identity is part of the buying decision.

nodes.name: Myojin Riki Seisakusho
nodes.specialty: Kochi workshop context for Myojin family polishing and finishing.

nodes.name: Naohito Myojin
nodes.specialty: High-end convex sharpening and polishing; Kyuzo and Myojin are treated here as buyer-facing names for the same Naohito Myojin profile.
nodes.caveat: Kyuzo, Myojin and Naohito Myojin are represented as the same person in this graph; verify exact disclosure per listing.

nodes.name: Toru Tamura
nodes.specialty: Blacksmith side of Tetsujin buyer discussions, commonly paired with Myojin polishing.
nodes.caveat: Community-heavy node: keep exact product attribution tied to retailer listings.

nodes.name: Tetsujin
nodes.specialty: Buyer-facing line identity where smith and polisher attribution matter.

nodes.name: Zakuri
nodes.specialty: Kochi/Tosa Knife Japan line for rustic carbon profiles, especially small working knives and single-bevel utility shapes.

nodes.name: Toyonaga Hamono
nodes.specialty: Tosa/Kochi Knife Japan maker context for specialty single-bevel and regional working profiles in Gingami #3 and Aogami #2.

nodes.name: Sasaoka Hasamiya
nodes.specialty: Tosa Knife Japan branch with Sanuki-no-Megumi and Tosa-no-Daichi product lines.

nodes.name: Katsuhide Kajiya
nodes.specialty: Kochi/Tosa workshop listed by Knife Japan; exact steel and profile claims should stay attached to individual listings.

nodes.name: Tosa blacksmith context
nodes.specialty: Regional context for Kochi/Tosa forged kitchen and utility knives.

edges.label: Myojin workshop
edges.detail: Naohito Myojin is represented inside the Myojin Riki Seisakusho workshop branch.

edges.label: Tamura / Myojin pairing
edges.detail: Tetsujin-style buying discussions commonly separate Tamura forging from Myojin polishing/finishing.

edges.label: Tetsujin smith trail
edges.detail: Tetsujin is linked to Tamura as the blacksmith side of the line trail.

edges.label: Tetsujin polish trail
edges.detail: Tetsujin is linked to Myojin because polishing identity is central to how buyers discuss the line.

edges.label: Tosa context
edges.detail: Tamura is placed in Kochi/Tosa context without implying a specific apprenticeship trail.

edges.label: Kochi / Tosa listing
edges.detail: Knife Japan places Zakuri in Kochi/Tosa context with traditional carbon working profiles.

edges.label: Kochi / Tosa listing
edges.detail: Knife Japan lists Toyonaga Hamono under Tosa/Kochi with specialty single-bevel and regional working knives.

edges.label: Tosa listing
edges.detail: Knife Japan lists Sasaoka Hasamiya in the Tosa branch with Sanuki-no-Megumi and Tosa-no-Daichi lines.

edges.label: Kochi / Tosa listing
edges.detail: Knife Japan lists Katsuhide Kajiya in the Kochi/Tosa branch.

regions.name: Miki / Hyogo
regions.summary: A non-Sakai example slot for Tanaka Uchihamono and the Kazumi Kawakita relationship request.
notes: This region is included because Adrian specifically asked for the Tanaka Uchihamono and Kazumi Kawakita relationship.
The graph marks the relationship without overstating exact line names until a stronger source is added.

nodes.name: Tanaka Uchihamono
nodes.specialty: Forged kitchen knives; read by exact smith, line, grind and retailer details.
nodes.caveat: Relationship included from Adrian's requested example; add exact retailer/source link when available.

nodes.name: Shigeki Tanaka
nodes.specialty: Miki-area blacksmith associated with Tanaka Uchihamono; Cleancut describes limited hand-forged production just outside Kobe, led by Shigeki after Kazuyuki Tanaka.

nodes.name: Katsuto Tanaka
nodes.specialty: Tanaka family/workshop context for Miki forged knives.
nodes.caveat: Family/workshop node added for genealogy context; Cleancut names Kazuyuki Tanaka as the father whose work Shigeki continued, while exact product claims still need listing checks.

edges.label: Tanaka workshop
edges.detail: Shigeki Tanaka is represented inside the Tanaka Uchihamono workshop branch.

edges.label: family lineage
edges.detail: The Tanaka family/workshop relationship is shown as genealogy context, not as a claim about every export line.

edges.label: working relationship
edges.detail: Added to make the Tanaka Uchihamono / Kazumi Kawakita relationship visible as requested.

regions.name: Seki / Gifu
regions.summary: Industrial and semi-industrial knife city context: production houses, export brands and stainless workhorses.
notes: Seki is useful for understanding reliable production knives, not only artisan smith/sharpener chains.
Read these nodes as brand and workshop context unless a listing names the exact maker or grinder.

nodes.name: Misono
nodes.specialty: Seki-made western-handled professional knives, especially UX10 and Swedish carbon lines.

nodes.name: MAC
nodes.specialty: Thin stamped/semi-stamped Japanese professional kitchen knives with high export visibility.

nodes.name: Masahiro
nodes.specialty: Seki production knives for professional kitchens, stainless workhorse context and asymmetrical edge discussions.
nodes.caveat: Source trail is community-led until an official English source is attached.

nodes.name: Glestain
nodes.specialty: Seki production knives with distinctive scalloped/granton-style blade faces.

nodes.name: Hattori
nodes.specialty: Seki custom and high-finish workshop context often discussed around FH and KD lines.
nodes.caveat: Community-heavy node; exact maker attribution should be tied to the specific line.

nodes.name: Suisin
nodes.specialty: Professional Japanese knife brand crossing Sakai and Seki buying conversations.

nodes.name: Moki
nodes.specialty: Seki/Gifu production brand listed by Hitohira, with outdoor, pocket and other edge tools alongside stainless steel product lines.

edges.label: Seki pro stainless comparison
edges.detail: Misono and MAC are both useful reference points for production Japanese professional knives.

edges.label: Seki workhorse comparison
edges.detail: Misono and Masahiro sit in the same practical Seki buying lane for western-handled pro knives.

edges.label: production geometry comparison
edges.detail: Glestain and MAC show two different Seki production approaches to food release and thinness.

edges.label: Seki production context
edges.detail: Moki is added as a Seki/Gifu production-brand context from Hitohira, not as a kitchen-knife-only comparison.

edges.label: high-finish Seki context
edges.detail: Hattori and Suisin are included as higher-finish Seki-adjacent buyer context, not a direct workshop relationship.

edges.label: western pro knife context
edges.detail: Suisin and Misono help frame the western-handled professional side of Japanese production knives.

regions.name: Tsubame / Niigata
regions.summary: Niigata production and small-workshop context outside the Sanjo blacksmith-forward cluster.
notes: This branch separates Tojiro/Tsubame production context from Sanjo handmade workhorse context.
Use it for stainless, factory-workshop and value-performance comparisons.

nodes.name: Tojiro
nodes.specialty: Tsubame/Niigata production knives and the common entry point for Japanese gyuto ownership.

nodes.name: Fujitora
nodes.specialty: Tojiro-linked domestic/export brand context for stainless production knives.

edges.label: brand family
edges.detail: Fujitora is shown as Tojiro-linked brand context for domestic/export naming.

regions.name: Kyoto
regions.summary: Kyoto-based contemporary maker context for individual craftspeople outside the large Sakai, Sanjo, Echizen and Seki clusters.
notes: Use this branch for Kyoto-based makers and sharpeners whose relationships still need stronger public source trails.
Relationships here should stay conservative and visibly caveated.

nodes.name: Ivan Fonseca
nodes.specialty: Kyoto-based contemporary maker/sharpener node added to the map for follow-up sourcing and relationship mapping.
nodes.caveat: Kyoto location added from Adrian's correction; public source trail and relationship edges still need stronger verification.

regions.name: Aomori / Hirosaki
regions.summary: Northern Japan workshop context for decorative forged lines and export-visible Damascus work.
notes: This branch starts with Nigara and should grow with exact smith, line and workshop relationships as sources are attached.

nodes.name: Nigara Hamono
nodes.specialty: Hirosaki workshop known for Anmon, Damascus, SG2/R2 and visually expressive forged lines.

nodes.name: Tsuyoshi Yoshizawa
nodes.specialty: Nigara Hamono maker node used to separate workshop identity from individual smith context.
nodes.caveat: Keep exact line attribution tied to product listings.

edges.label: Nigara workshop
edges.detail: Tsuyoshi Yoshizawa is represented inside the Nigara Hamono workshop context.

regions.name: Okayama / Niimi
regions.summary: Takeda-focused regional branch for highly distinctive forged geometry and AS buying context.
notes: Takeda should be read as its own geometry and grind conversation rather than a generic carbon knife node.

nodes.name: Takeda Hamono
nodes.specialty: Distinctive tall, thin, forged knives with kurouchi character and Aogami Super buyer lore.

nodes.name: Shosui Takeda
nodes.specialty: Blacksmith identity behind Takeda's highly recognizable profiles and forged geometry.

nodes.name: Otsuki Hamono
nodes.specialty: Rural Okayama father-and-son workshop context from Knife Japan, with kitchen and agricultural tools, kurouchi work and final sharpness emphasized.

nodes.name: Bizen Osafune Nihon-to
nodes.specialty: Okayama listing from Knife Japan, included as Bizen/Osafune regional context rather than a Takeda relationship.

edges.label: Takeda workshop
edges.detail: Shosui Takeda is represented inside the Takeda Hamono workshop context.

edges.label: Okayama makers
edges.detail: Otsuki Hamono is added as a separate Okayama workshop from Knife Japan, not as a Takeda lineage claim.

edges.label: Okayama listing
edges.detail: Knife Japan lists Bizen Osafune Nihon-to in Okayama; this edge marks regional proximity, not lineage.

regions.name: Kumamoto
regions.summary: Moritaka-focused branch for long-running carbon forging, AS lines and rustic workhorse discussions.
notes: Moritaka is included as a regional specialist branch; expand with exact generations and line relationships as stronger sources are added.

nodes.name: Moritaka Hamono
nodes.specialty: Kumamoto forge known for Aogami Super, carbon workhorse profiles and long-running family workshop context.

nodes.name: Minomo Hamono
nodes.specialty: Kumamoto workshop listed by Knife Japan; exact smith, steel and line details should be checked on individual product pages.

nodes.name: Okahide Hamono
nodes.specialty: Kumamoto workshop listed by Knife Japan; useful as a regional reference for smaller everyday knives.

regions.name: Kagoshima
regions.summary: Knife Japan regional branch for southern Kyushu makers that need individual product-page follow-up.
notes: This branch currently uses Knife Japan's maker/region listing as the source trail; add individual profiles as stronger sources are found.

nodes.name: Fukamizu Hamono
nodes.specialty: Kagoshima workshop listed by Knife Japan; exact line, steel and smith details should be verified per listing.

regions.name: Nagasaki
regions.summary: Knife Japan branch for Nagasaki workshops and smith identities outside the larger Sakai/Sanjo/Echizen clusters.
notes: Use this as a source-led regional bucket until stronger maker biographies are attached.

nodes.name: Hayashida Hamono
nodes.specialty: Nagasaki workshop listed by Knife Japan; exact product attribution should stay with individual listings.

nodes.name: Mori Kajiya
nodes.specialty: Nagasaki workshop listed by Knife Japan.

nodes.name: Shigemitsu Hamono
nodes.specialty: Nagasaki workshop listed by Knife Japan.

nodes.name: Yoshimitsu Kajiya
nodes.specialty: Nagasaki smith/workshop listed by Knife Japan; homepage examples include tall nakiri context.

nodes.name: Yoshimitsu Hamono
nodes.specialty: Nagasaki workshop listed by Knife Japan; keep separate from Yoshimitsu Kajiya unless a stronger source ties them together.

regions.name: Yamaguchi
regions.summary: Knife Japan regional branch for Yamaguchi-listed workshops.
notes: Use individual Knife Japan product pages before making line or steel claims.

nodes.name: Homi Kajiya
nodes.specialty: Yamaguchi workshop listed by Knife Japan.

regions.name: Tanegashima
regions.summary: Knife Japan branch for Tanegashima Island tool and knife context.
notes: Knife Japan lists Ikenami Hamono under Tanegashima Island, including scissors/shears context.

nodes.name: Ikenami Hamono
nodes.specialty: Tanegashima Island workshop listed by Knife Japan, with scissors/shears and kitchen knife context.

regions.name: Saga
regions.summary: Knife Japan regional branch for Saga-listed workshops.
notes: Knife Japan homepage examples include Kawasaki Kajiya oyster knife context.

nodes.name: Kawasaki Kajiya
nodes.specialty: Saga workshop listed by Knife Japan; homepage examples include recycled-steel oyster knife context.

regions.name: Hiroshima
regions.summary: Knife Japan regional branch for Hiroshima-listed workshops.
notes: Current node is source-led from Knife Japan's brand list; expand with product-page details later.

nodes.name: Mikami Hamono
nodes.specialty: Hiroshima workshop listed by Knife Japan.

regions.name: Shimane
regions.summary: Knife Japan branch for Shimane makers and Yasugi steel context.
notes: Knife Japan highlights Yasugi specialty steel context around Moriya Munemitsu YHC and lists Unshu makers in the same prefecture.

nodes.name: Moriya Munemitsu YHC
nodes.specialty: Shimane workshop listed by Knife Japan, with homepage context around Yasugi specialty steels.

nodes.name: Unshu Chuzen Hamono
nodes.specialty: Shimane workshop listed by Knife Japan.

nodes.name: Unshu Yukimitsu Hamono
nodes.specialty: Shimane workshop listed by Knife Japan; homepage copy frames the branch around traditional sharpness from the home of Japanese steel.

regions.name: Miyazaki
regions.summary: Knife Japan regional branch for Miyazaki-listed workshops.
notes: Nakamura Hamono appears in both Miyazaki and Oita Knife Japan listings; keep regional nodes separate until product pages clarify the relationship.

nodes.name: Nakamura Hamono
nodes.specialty: Miyazaki listing from Knife Japan; represented separately from the Oita listing until a stronger source connects them.

nodes.name: Yamamoto Hamono
nodes.specialty: Miyazaki workshop listed by Knife Japan.

regions.name: Oita
regions.summary: Knife Japan regional branch for Oita-listed workshops.
notes: Nakamura Hamono also appears in Miyazaki; this node remains source-specific until a better relationship trail is added.

nodes.name: Nakamura Hamono
nodes.specialty: Oita listing from Knife Japan; represented separately from the Miyazaki listing until product-page evidence connects them.

regions.name: Tokushima
regions.summary: Knife Japan regional branch for Tokushima-listed workshops.
notes: Knife Japan popular listings include Okubo Kajiya nakiri and santoku examples.

nodes.name: Okubo Kajiya
nodes.specialty: Tokushima workshop listed by Knife Japan; homepage examples include Aogami #2 nakiri and santoku.

regions.name: Tottori
regions.summary: Knife Japan branch for Tottori workshops and handle context.
notes: Knife Japan homepage highlights Otsuka Hamono with custom mountain cherry handles.

nodes.name: Otsuka Hamono
nodes.specialty: Tottori workshop listed by Knife Japan; homepage copy highlights custom mountain cherry handles.

nodes.name: Yashima Nogu Kogyo
nodes.specialty: Tottori workshop listed by Knife Japan.

regions.name: Fukuoka
regions.summary: Knife Japan regional branch for Fukuoka-listed makers.
notes: Knife Japan homepage popular listings include Shiro Kunimitsu yanagiba context.

nodes.name: Shiro Kunimitsu
nodes.specialty: Fukuoka maker listed by Knife Japan; homepage examples include Kurouchi Special Yanagiba.

regions.name: Nagano
regions.summary: Hitohira/Jiro context for mountain-forged, low-volume knives and product stories around outdoor cooking.
notes: Hitohira does not sell Jiro knives directly on its own site; it points buyers to Jiro retailers.
The Jiro BBQ knife story is included as product-story context, not proof of every Jiro knife's use case.

nodes.name: Jiro
nodes.specialty: Low-volume Jiro Tsuchime knives with Hitohira retailer-routing context and a BBQ knife story tied to mountain cooking in Nagano.
nodes.caveat: Hitohira confirms the collection and retailer route; exact workshop biography and individual knife specs should be checked listing by listing.

regions.name: Mie
regions.summary: Hitohira branch for Daitoku and small old-style blacksmith context outside the usual knife-tourist regions.
notes: Daitoku is included from Hitohira's brand-story text as a small blacksmith story, not as a broad regional school.

nodes.name: Daitoku
nodes.specialty: Hitohira-listed blacksmith story: a fifth-generation family context, old-style local-order work and hand-finished edges, with bread knife work highlighted.
nodes.caveat: Source text is Hitohira's brand story; add independent sources before mapping teacher/student relationships.

regions.name: Tokyo
regions.summary: Tokyo maker and retailer-brand context for highly opinionated carbon and professional house-line knives.
notes: Tokyo includes both maker identities and retailer/supplier identities; those are different buying clues.
Hitohira, Morihei, Hi-Condition and Tsubaya are included as source/curation nodes rather than claimed blacksmith nodes.

nodes.name: Teruyasu Fujiwara
nodes.specialty: Highly distinctive carbon and stainless-clad knives with strong community discussion around grind variance and performance.

nodes.name: Hitohira Tokyo
nodes.specialty: Tokyo retail and wholesale operation for Japanese knives, tools and stones; Hitohira describes in-store sharpening and repair workshops.

nodes.name: Morihei
nodes.specialty: Asakusabashi, Tokyo knife and whetstone supplier with a long history of relationships with blacksmiths, sharpeners and natural stone makers.

nodes.name: HI-CONDITION
nodes.specialty: Asakusabashi, Tokyo brand context listed by Hitohira; useful as a retail/source node rather than a single-maker claim.

nodes.name: Cutlery Tsubaya
nodes.specialty: Tokyo cutlery-store context listed by Hitohira; source-led node for retailer curation and house-line discovery.

nodes.name: Kanatoko
nodes.specialty: Hitohira-listed brand node added for follow-up sourcing and line mapping.
nodes.caveat: Hitohira confirms the brand listing; region and maker-story details need stronger source attachment.

edges.label: Tokyo source context
edges.detail: Hitohira and Morihei both sit in Tokyo as curation/supplier nodes rather than simple smith nodes.

edges.label: Asakusabashi context
edges.detail: Hitohira lists Morihei and HI-CONDITION with Asakusabashi, Tokyo context.

edges.label: Tokyo retail context
edges.detail: Tsubaya is included as a Tokyo cutlery-store and curation node from Hitohira's brand index.
